sentences.text: My name is the Regina Zielinski.
And it's R-E-G-I-N-A, and Zielinski is Z-I-E-L-I-N-S-K-I.
And my maiden name is Feldman, F-E-L-D-M-A-N.
The 2nd to 9, September, 1925.
In another few months, it will be 70.
[LAUGHS]
It's a small town between two big cities, between Lublin and Chelm and was called Siedliszcze.
And it is S-I-E-D-L-I-S-Z-C-Z-E.
Poland.
--and Chelm, yes.
Oh, yes yes, yes.
We-- buses used to run to Chelm and Lublin because the railway station was a little bit too far away.
It was only 51 kilometers to Lublin and 24 to Chelm, but different directions.
My father's name was Joseph, and my mother's, Golda-- pardon me-- and, how shall I say, a Jewish home.

But we were living on the outskirts of the town because my father had a building business-- building material business, and we were so integrated between the-- the Catholics and the Lutheranians and the-- the-- the Seventh-day Adventists.
We had only one very big school, and there was a-- a Orthodox-- Russian Orthodox.
We were all in the same school.
It was a primary school.
It was sometime, I think, about a thousands kids in that school.
The school was converted after the First World War.
It was a castle, and the one who owned that castle and the surrounding-- surrounding, they didn't have any heirs, so they gave it to the town.
And they made out a school-- school for it.
It was a beautiful, beautiful school.
Yes.
I couldn't recall much till about '37, 1937.
We have had heard that in bigger cities, they had pickets standing, and they had the Jewish shops.
They were not allowed like to go and buy from Jews and all that.

But one thing I remember that my father was not allowed to buy-- not-- not-- that not allowed, but he couldn't get really nice timber for doors and other-- and furniture from a-- not a [INAUDIBLE], from the Polish saw mills, government saw mills, I'd rather the say.
And my father had to get a partner.
And then it was a suited partner with a Polish partner.
We were able to obtain that material from the government's saw mills.
But there were other saw mills, but they didn't have as good quality timber as those, yes, and that's when we knew there was something going on.

And then we heard about-- please forgive me-- about Hitler and about anti-Semitism because a lot of that started of a lot of people were sent over back from Germany to Poland, the one who were born in Poland had to come back.
And then anti-Semitism, but they are-- but where I was I didn't feel it at all because we were with the kids used to come.
We used to celebrate Christmas, and they helped us to celebrate Hanukkah and-- and then Pesach and all that.
It wasn't--
Yes, not in school, at home.
They used to come to our home.
And they couldn't understand why they are eating at our house things and we were not allowed anything from them to eat there.
So that was the only-- only difference.
You know, we had to explain.
No, we were only admiring.
We could have the lollies or an apple from the tree, but we were only admiring.
And we had, at school, a very big Christmas tree.
And we had in the middle of the town, a big Christmas tree and all that.
No, no, only the last two-- three-- three-- two or three years until the war broke out.
And then there was nothing left.
You know.
The German took everything.
Yes.
Yes, Grandmother and Great-grandmother.
The house was divided like in half.
They had smaller quarters, then it was five-- five children with us.
So we had a bit more in the house, yes.
My mother's--
Yes, the other grandparents lived more in the middle of the town.
Yes.
My father's--
Oh, yes, very often.
No, our-- our home--
--because my mother's sister sometimes used to come over for Pesach.
They lived in a different city.
So.
My eldest brother was Max, and he was born in 1921.
And then my sister was born in 1923, and her name was Friedl.
And then I was the middle one, and after me, there was a brother was born in 1930--
--'32 No, the youngest was in '32.
And after me, the brother was in '28, and his brother was Teodor.
His name was Teodor.
And the youngest was Paul.
He was born in 1932.
Oh, because my father had to travel a lot to buy material for it, and my mother was mostly just selling a person or to attend to it.
It was a big concern.
So.
The office my brother was starting in accountancy, then my eldest brother, so he helped a lot.
And--
It was where the house-- the house was in the front and the whole yard was in the back.
And when the yard finished with the building sheds and all that, we had a very big garden.
And in the middle-- in the garden we had a lot of trees like an orchard.
And that finished off near a creek, so we had about-- it might be an acre or some-- more than an acre.
It was more than an acre, yes.
We were-- yes, we were successful.
It wasn't easy, but we were managed, yes.
Everybody, yes.
Yes, yes.
And we had a lot of from the community, like Polish friends.
They were always the best of friends with the-- with the mayor and with the-- the police and all that because from time to time, you know, we used to celebrate things.
They used to come over to our place, and we knew everybody, all the teachers, all of the-- you know, it was--
At home, they were, when my parents didn't want us to understand, or grandparents, used to speak amongst themself Russian.
But we used to pick up a few words.
But mostly was with the elder people, it was Yiddish.
And then amongst us, mostly Polish amongst us, yes.
Yes.
I wanted to be a chemist, pharmacist, because when I used to go to the pharmacist to get some medicine, I never could get out from there because I loved it that much.
But one consolation after when I came out here-- it might be strange to say that-- in the beginning, my daughter did it for me.
She's a pharmacist.
Yes, yes.
I don't know.
I had such a longing to-- to pick medicine.
And I was always sort of [INAUDIBLE] but careful, when it was sent the-- the pharmacist wasn't far away where we lived, about a couple of houses apart.
You know.
So it was really-- the whole surrounding of it.
And I used to-- it was different, the pharmacies, then it is now because they had to make all the medicine on-- on the premises.
It wasn't like everything was wrapped up.
All we used to have ready made was maybe Aspirin or tablets, certain tablets.
But all other things had to be made at the-- the premises, at the pharmacy.
No, no, no I was sitting on the bench, and I watched how he was doing it.
[LAUGHS]
No, actually his origination was Italian.
But they was Polish, you know.
I don't know how many generations, but his name was different.
Oh, yes.
Yes, yes.
There was quite a lot of Jewish students in there, or kids.
You know.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
The girls, no.
Only one year before the war, we got a teacher from that city, Lutsk, L-U-C-K.
And she taught.
We had the Sunday School, and that was like the-- the cheder for the girls.
Yes.
Oh, yes, a little bit of Hebrew.
And they taught us, you know, the Jewish alphabet and then to write and songs and all that.
But one year, we didn't-- then the war broke out.
And it wasn't much.
At home.
At home.
We heard it at night time.
It was a Thursday, and we heard that something is going on.
We called all the-- what do you call the-- the reserve, the soldiers in reserve were all called up, sometimes in June, July, or August.
And usually they used to go once a year for six weeks training, but they never came back.
And then we heard that we were all standing near the police station.
It was about maybe 12 o'clock at night.
I can't remember what time it was.
I was-- was a little bit, but not much because my elder the brother and then sister, they were more-- and I was still a bit too-- [BACKROUND NOISE] too young for it.
But I used to trot along with them.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
We used to go in May, the month, May.
We used to get up early in the morning, about 5 o'clock, and used to go hiking.
That was the most beautiful time, you know, before school because school used to start at 8 o'clock.
So we had a kilometer to walk to school, yes.
Oh, I don't remember.
It must have been Habonim, because the Zionists, we are all connected with the Zionists and with the songs and-- and the uniform.
And I remember, you know, it looked a little bit like the scouts, but it wasn't.
You know, the-- you know, and the flag, the Jewish flag, and we learned sing Hatikvah and all that, so yes.

In the town, what happened, first of all in the beginning, we had a lot of-- we saw the Polish army was matching through the state one day, and then a lot of in the cavalry and artillery was marching.
So they were going to concentrate on the River Bug.
That was a very big-- big river.
But in the meantime, we heard that the Russian has-- have crossed the border, and they took all the Polish army away.
And then they came in the 18th of September, the Russian army came in to-- to [INAUDIBLE].
And it was then-- I don't remember.
It was the first day of Rosh Hashanah or the second.
[SPEAKING RUSSIAN] .
What is it?
You know.
Oh, yes.
We opened the doors.
They came in and all that, so they was stationed there.
I don't remember exactly how many days, and then for a-- for a week or a fortnight, not just there but all around, and then they withdrew.
They probably went to that River Bug, and that's where they stopped.
And it was a lawless time.

The hooligans, they were-- they were not just, you know, whatever they could lay their hands on, stealing, and-- and even they got from the Polish army, from the one who were left running or something, the ammunition and all that.
And a delegate from the town went to Chelm to ask the Germans to come in to have a little bit of order because we were hiding in the cellars.
We were afraid to go out because it was a lawless period.
So-- but when the Germans came, [BACKROUND NOISE] we didn't realize that it's going to be even worse.
It wasn't lawless with the Germans.
It wasn't so bad right away, but it started off then.
When they first arrived, they took away the, how shall I say, they took away some teachers.
They took away the mayor of the town.
They took away prominent people, even priests.
And they saw that they are going to be hostages.
And they will return, but nothing will happen, but nobody will harm the-- the military-- the German military.
But we never saw them.
That stopped.
Oh, yes.
Yes, yes, yes, Polish
Because a lot of the Jewish young people went with the Russians.
When the Russians withdrew, they went with them, yes.
No, but then it was all right when the German arrived, but they were starting to take things away, whatever we-- we-- we had, like from the business and all that.
So--
The Germans, they didn't believe anything, whatever, but they didn't-- we were not evacuated from-- from the place, from the house.
We still lived there, but we had to give up a half for somebody else, like a veteran, a surgeon come to live and be here.
Only half of a [INAUDIBLE].
No, no, no, no, Polish men, a Polish veterinary surgeon.
Yes, yes, everything.
No, no, no, no, no.
Later on when the winter came, I had to stand in a queue for a bucket of coal, our own coal.
And that was a ration and how far did the bucket of-- of coal go?
But to be somehow managed, you know, with-- with-- we had a little bit of food stacked away and a lot of other things, and we managed.
The Germans took away our horse, and that was something like a member of the family.
We used to ride [INAUDIBLE].
I remember, I couldn't reach when I was little, so we made a little ladder.
So I used to climb up on the horse on a ladder.
[LAUGHS] But later on, about the first winter, it wasn't so, so bad.
But the second winter, '40, like '39 and '40, and that I can't remember so very-- one German soldier was on a motorbike, and he ran into a ditch or something and he killed himself.
And the whole town was to blame.
So they took all the men out.
It didn't matter, Jews, non-Jews Jews, and they were taking away every 10th person.
Yes, and we never saw them.
No.
We heard, you know, that they maybe sent to camps.
And somebody used to say that they were executed in some forest.
But I was too young then to-- to-- to try to-- to-- to know everything, what was going on.
It was a very, very harsh time.
Yes.
Well, we still-- we had to go out for work, whatever was--
No, first of all, the Germans said that we can't go back to school because the Jews will have separate schools.
And we never had separate schools.
But we were taken out to work.
Yes, yes.
And we would-- whatever could, like over 15, and 15 or something, we had to go out of the town to work.
That was like straighten out rivers.
We had to dig ditches.
We had to make dams to keep the water, and then, you know, all that.
I don't know what for, but we were work.
I had to work.
And after that, in '41--
Yes.
Mm-hmm.
Father, the youngest was too young, but they used to come with us.
You know.
And sometimes they--
My mother didn't come because she was providing and making food during the day.
But my father-- all of us, especially most the young people, had to-- they had to come to work.
And then the 18th of May, '41.
Yes--
I don't want to--
Went to work.
Yes, see we still-- we were not far.
We were still in the house, and we had still a big garden and-- and the orchards.
And they didn't take that away because it was still growing.
While we were at the home, we planted a lot of things.
And there was still, you know--
No, no, no, not livestock.
No livestock, no.
But we we're given, sometimes, milk.
But that was without cream.
What do you call that milk?
Like light, like they have it here now.
Skim milk, yes.
And then there were other things we cooked.
You know.
The farmers used to come along, and we had money so we were able to buy and all that, but we didn't starve.
No.
Yes.
That was a hard winter.
It was very, very hard.
Oh, I can't remember because we still were able to bake bread-- bread, and that was the most important thing.
And then to get some-- some flour or we used to go to the mill and get something from them because we had a lot of farmers who were friend of ours.
And maybe a bit of grain and all that, so we-- once we had flour and maybe beans and then other things and-- and all that, so it wasn't too bad, you know.
We could go out to-- to-- we had money to buy, still, milk or cheese and all that.
It was not easy, but we just managed.
You know.
But then the 18th of May, that was '41, we went to work.
And when we came back, we were told not to go home anymore.
My mother, my grandmother, my great grandmother, and neighbor, Mrs.
Kalb, ?] they were hidden at our place.
We had a double wall in the-- in the barn where-- where the horse and the cow was.
So it was a double wall, and it was made up so that it didn't look like a double wall.
And they were hiding there because they were too old to go to work or not feeling well, [? anyhow ?] grandmother and parents.
And all the elderly people and all the children who were at homes, at homes, they were taken away, and never saw them anymore.
And from that day on, we had to be in the ghetto.
No, no, we-- we later on learned--
--after the Germans, you know, went away in the ghetto, we-- we couldn't go back to live at home.
And my parents, the father was with us, but my mother, grandmother, great grandmother, they come into to ghetto, and Mrs.
Kalb ?] found a family too.
And we were living the ghetto, but the ghetto was very small.
They took a couple of streets.
They-- they-- they-- they just divided things, and that's where you have to stay.
There wasn't many left.
There were only maybe about 700 people, and I don't know how many houses.
But--
Yes, later on when it was a little bit eased up, and we were allowed to go because it was not far from the garden where we had, so we were allowed to sneak in and get something from the garden.
The ghetto, we had only one room and a kitchen and a little shed.
And that was all.
But we managed to, you know, to have some straw mattresses and all that at night time to put on the floor where to sleep and all that.
So fuel was very scarce.
But I don't know how my parents managed, but they managed somehow.
The house, no-- the-- no, the family home, the [INAUDIBLE] took it.
Yes, and they-- they had the secretaries stay there, and some offices was all for the [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, that was dissolved straight away when the-- when the war broke out.
You know, that was-- everything was taken away.
Maybe he got something out of it, but I wasn't old enough to-- to inquire about things like that yet.
And then we stayed there until the 22nd of October, '42.
But before that, they burned down the-- the synagogue before we got to the ghetto.
And-- and--
No, no, it would've been outside of the ghetto, not far from the police station on the main road.
They did-- they did it in only private houses.
And then they knew that the rabbi was there, and then they knew when it was Yom Kippur.
And that was the first raid that they took a lot of men away.
That was when the synagogue was-- was burned down.
And we used the people--
No, that was-- yeah, when Yom Kippur or-- or Rosh Hashanah [INAUDIBLE] and that the Germans knew--
--when they were all-- all together.
And then in '42, we were already in the ghetto.
Oh, yes, yes.
Every morning, the same sort of work.
It was so hard during the winter because when the soil was frozen.
And not even during the summer when I just got my portion of digging it, it was clay.
It wasn't easy.
If I got sand, it was all right.
Sometimes there were lots of rocks, and the boys used to help-- help out.
There was sense, because there was a very nice, big river was running.
And the river didn't run straight, only with a lot of curves and a lot of things.

And they wanted that river so the-- a lot of them [? pastures ?] There's a lot of the-- the-- want it to dry out, so there would be more length for the farmers or maybe a lot of the-- the Germans, they were taking everything over and just to make our life misery, and that-- that's what we went to work for.
Oh, yes, yes, yes.
They probably, you know, maybe Saturday they used to get together somewhere for-- for Friday night.

And it wasn't much of a celebration then or-- for holidays, but the Rosh Hashanah, Erev Rosh Hashanah, my eldest brother passed away, and it was so tragic because all day he was cleaning the bricks from the synagogue.
It was a very hot day.
It was September-- the 11th of September.
And when he came home, he didn't feel well.
And it was-- the next day was Rosh Hashanah, and he went to bed early.
And then he got the middle about 2 o'clock in the morning a heart attack.
And we were not allowed to go out.
We had to wait for the sentry to come along with the German soldier as police or whatever they were, and then we could go and call a doctor.
But by the time we come along, there were not any way to get a-- to get out to-- to go even to call somebody over to go and fetch the doctor.
By the time the doctor came, he couldn't help him anymore.
Oh, no hospital, no.

Even if there would have been a hospital, if the doctor would've come early enough, he could have helped him because we had, from Austria, doctors, you know, all the people who were evacuated from-- from Germany or the, you know, during the war.
And the Austrian doctor was still in the-- he was in the ghetto too.
He said, if he would have been able to come earlier, but we didn't know about him.
He said, he had very high blood pressure, and that's what-- it killed him.
He was very tall.
And that maybe was, you know-- he was standing, going for work, everybody could see him.
He was over-- over, you know, 6 foot, so that's-- he didn't survive.
The-- the-- still the cemetery was still intact, so he had a Jewish burial.
And the rabbi was [INAUDIBLE].
It was quickly because Rosh Hashanah was-- was the eve of Rosh Hashanah.
And--
It feel that my sister got so very sick.
She held it more than my mother because my mother had to help us to get over it.
It was very hard.
But then it wasn't long after, we were taken away to that camp-- to that labor camp.
Two [? months-- ?] October, the 22nd of October.
Labor camp, Staw.
S-T-A-W.
It was a very big mill, and we were-- they-- we were housed there, in the mill.
About 700-- 700 people were housed in that mill.
And there was, again, very hard work there.
Mostly the same thing, but we were working at home and at [?
Mogilanka ?] River.
And--
Sometimes [? other ?] things, yes.
Oh, by Germans, yes.
By the Germans, yes.
But--
Oh, they didn't beat us up or something like that, but the-- the food, we-- we didn't get much to eat, only the morning some bread and maybe coffee.
They called it coffee.
And when we were coming home, there was a bit of soup.
But there was an incident in December.
That is something that I don't know how to get it out to my mind.
We were standing-- it is Sunday.
It was a Sunday, and we were not working on Sunday.
We had to get everything like ready to get clean to-- to wash your bed and all that.
It wasn't very easy, but to somehow we tried, you know, with snow to melt snow if there wasn't enough water.
And all of a sudden, we saw two black dots behind the fences of the camp.
And they were coming closer.
And all of a sudden, we heard two children calling for their parents.
And father-- the grandfather and the parents, they were the twins.
They were probably really left with some people to-- to look after them or something.
And the two dogs tore the kids apart while the parents and all of us were standing on this side of the fence, and we couldn't help.
So then I knew from that day on what the Germans are capable of.
I still took it a little bit light because my mother remembered the First World War how the German soldiers helped-- helped them when the Russians withdrew and all of that.
She couldn't believe that from angels in 20 years could turn out to be such-- I don't know how to call them because if I call them beasts, I will insult the beast.
Because a beast only kills when he's hungry or when he is attacked.
But it was worse.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So and then not too long after, the 20's of December, we told we were not going to work anymore.
We had to stay and beg, and farmers come along with horse-driven wagons.
And we were taken out from that camp from Staw.
Oh, no.
We were driven.
It wasn't that far.
We were driven with-- on horse-driven wagons.
We were-- we didn't walk.
And we were told to take-- we didn't have much of belongings anymore in the ghetto because we couldn't even go back to get clothes-- anything from the house, but we managed, you know.
People-- the secretaries, we knew them very well.
They had to helped us a lot because, for one time, they asked me or my sister to come and clean the houses, and then they were giving us a little bit of clothes, what we had in the house.
So it wasn't-- we helped ourselves, you know.
There were a lot of good people around.
And then the 20s of December, '42.
That morning, it was such a bleak morning.
The whole world was covered with snow.
Mm-hmm.
